humanities_role_explanation: Evaluates LLMs on the library metadata science task of automated subject cataloging (Museology/Information Science) using records that span both scientific and humanities domains like Architecture, History, Linguistics, and Literature.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Bilingual evaluation using text-only inputs consisting of bibliographic titles and abstracts in English and German to assign taxonomic labels.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Predicting and ranking a customized top-k list of relevant GND subject headings from a massive vocabulary of topical terms based on record text.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: It is a specialized domain-specific benchmark focused on automated subject tagging for library catalog systems.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluated multiple generative LLMs including GPT-4o, Llama-3/3.1/3.2, Qwen2.5, ChatGLM 4, and Mistral-7B.

llm_is_primary_system.note: The primary objective is evaluating the viability and effectiveness of LLMs compared to traditional classification systems for library indexing.

arts_humanities_data.note: Includes record indexing from humanities disciplines including Architecture, Linguistics, Literature Studies, and History, utilizing the GND authority file.

quantitative_metrics.note: Evaluated quantitatively using Precision@k, Recall@k, and F1-score@k, supplemented by qualitative expert manual evaluation.

english_language.note: The paper is written in English and the benchmark includes English along with German records.

summary: This paper presents SemEval-2025 Task 5: LLMs4Subjects, a shared task designed to benchmark LLM-based automated subject indexing systems using the German National Library's GND taxonomy. The benchmark utilizes over 123,000 bilingual (English/German) records from the German National Library for Science and Technology (TIB). The task compares generative LLMs, dense retrieval/embedding methods, and traditional extreme multi-label classification techniques using both quantitative metrics and professional qualitative evaluations.
key_findings: In the quantitative evaluations, the Annif toolkit ensemble achieved the highest overall Recall@k of 0.63 on the all-subjects dataset, while the RUC Team's embedding matching approach led the tib-core dataset with an overall Recall@k of 0.66. In the qualitative evaluations conducted by library specialists, the DNB-AI-Project system, which used few-shot LLM prompting with an ensemble of Llama models and no fine-tuning, achieved the top performance with an average Recall@k of 0.51, showcasing the strong zero-shot generalization capabilities of LLMs over heavily fine-tuned systems.
